pred_reason: The SWC-101 label applies due to integer overflow/underflow vulnerabilities found in unprotected arithmetic operations across multiple functions, including calculatePayoutForAddress. The SWC-107 label fits because the reentrancy vulnerability in the withdraw function violates the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern by performing external transfers before state updates. Evidence: Unguarded arithmetic in calculatePayoutForAddress() without SafeMath (Finding 3); Reentrancy in withdraw() with transfer before state update (Finding 1); Unsafe state variable updates in deposit/withdraw without overflow checks (Finding 4)

findings.Description: Critical reentrancy vulnerability in CrowdWallet.withdraw() function where external call (transfer) is made before state updates, violating Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Allows malicious contracts to re-enter and drain the entire ETH balance from the contract

findings.Issue: Missing Access Control on startNewPayoutPeriod Function
findings.Description: Critical administrative function startNewPayoutPeriod() lacks onlyOwner modifier, allowing anyone to trigger payout period transitions. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Unauthorized users can manipulate payout timing, disrupt payout schedule, and potentially cause denial of service

findings.Description: Critical arithmetic operations in calculatePayoutForAddress() function performed without SafeMath protection, vulnerable to integer overflows. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential for payout calculation manipulation, contract fund drainage, or incorrect distribution amounts

findings.Description: Critical state variables lifetimeDeposits and lifetimePayouts updated using native arithmetic instead of SafeMath, vulnerable to overflow. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential for accounting corruption, incorrect financial reporting, and contract malfunction
findings.Location: CrowdWallet.deposit() and withdraw() functions, lines approximately 245 and 146

findings.Description: GenesisToken.giveTokens() function allows owner to mint unlimited tokens without supply cap, enabling token inflation. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Complete devaluation of token economy, loss of value for all token holders, economic manipulation

findings.Issue: Arithmetic Precision Loss in Payout Calculations
findings.Description: Integer division truncation in calculatePayoutForAddress() causes precision loss, unfairly excluding small token holders from payouts. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Small token holders receive zero payouts despite legitimate claims, unfair distribution system

findings.Description: Payout calculations based on current token balance allow users to front-run withdrawals by acquiring more tokens. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Users can manipulate payout amounts by timing token acquisitions, extracting more than fair share

findings.Description: transferOwnership() function doesn't properly revert on zero address input, only silently skips assignment. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Failed ownership transfers without clear feedback, potential operational confusion and inefficiencies

raw.pred_reason: The SWC-101 label applies due to integer overflow/underflow vulnerabilities found in unprotected arithmetic operations across multiple functions, including calculatePayoutForAddress. The SWC-107 label fits because the reentrancy vulnerability in the withdraw function violates the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern by performing external transfers before state updates. Evidence: Unguarded arithmetic in calculatePayoutForAddress() without SafeMath (Finding 3); Reentrancy in withdraw() with transfer before state update (Finding 1); Unsafe state variable updates in deposit/withdraw without overflow checks (Finding 4)
